Now, first thing I wanna do is have a caveat that sometimes you have to do things that don't scale. And especially at the start of your business, it's just that's the way to get started. you're just doing them to create an impact, to create the impression you want, the positioning you want, the service level you want. This is the idea that your strength eventually becomes your weakness. And that might sound a little weird, but let me explain. So for me, like one of the things that I'm strongest at is marketing. I'm really good at putting together marketing campaigns, putting together launches, putting together sales processes. It's just something that just comes naturally to me. I'm one of the very best in the world. When it comes to launches, I'm probably the best in the world. So it's a huge strength, but then as the business grows, and we start to grow the team and start to hire people, it's hard to hire people that are as good or better than me when I'm, if not the best in the world, one of the very best in the world. But then eventually the business grows to the level where I become a bottleneck. Because even though I'm really, really great at it, there's just me, one person, and I become the bottleneck. That goes from being the strength to being the weakness. If it's a strength in me doing it like one campaign every other month or several times a year, but all of a sudden the business has grown to the point where we need a new campaign every two weeks, and I can't do that, I become the bottleneck. I become the choke point. I constrain what the team can do. And it's hard for me to hire because I'm so good at it, it's hard to find someone to perform at my level. So you have to watch this, that your strength becomes your weakness, and you have to try to get out of it, and you have to try to hire for it. ## [18:09 - 21:12]

In that case, you could call that an upsell, although it wasn't technically an upsell, we made that upsell immediately. It was generally that offers made months after people go through product launch formula. Now, one of the things that comes a lot, this isn't technically how you scale or how to get leverage, but as you grow your business, you need to think about risk because every business has risk points. And they can be hard to see. Like if you look at Kodak, I mean the dominance of Kodak in the film industry, in the photography industry is hard to overstate. They just completely dominated. And then when digital photography came around, they were very slow to jump on board and they're gone now. The company is gone. They went from being the absolute dominant player in a huge industry to non-existence in, I don't know, a decade, two decades max. So risk points can be hard to see. So when I think about risk, one of the things I like to look at in my business, one of the ways that I get at the idea of where are my risk points is I periodically take some time, take a little retreat and I say, sit down. Why, if I was looking to buy a business and I came across my current business,

## [21:12 - 22:20]

and I didn't own it, but I just came across in a parallel universe, I've came across myself trying to sell my business, why would I object to buying it? Why would I not wanna buy it? And so that's something that, because every business has risk points. Every business has weaknesses, every business. And your business is no different and my business is no different.
